coredata.dc:title: A Schiff base-functionalized graphene quantum dot nanocomposite for preferable picric acid sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yuting
Tao, Tao
Wang, Huijun
Liu, Zeyu
Huang, Wei
Cao, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Fast and effective picric acid (PA) sensing in water and the air is urgently demanded environmental safety. Herein, a new sensor was designed and synthesized mediated by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for explosive detection. The luminescent Schiff base-functionalized GQDs (Schiff-GQDs) was immobilized on nanocomposite through in-situ electrospun. The quantitative experiment shows that the fluorescent quantum yield of Schiff-GQDs is 11.63%, four times higher than that of N-GQDs (2.71%) and seven times higher than that of dye 1 (1.51%). Luminescent Schiff-GQDs have a bigger quenching percentage, better selective detection of PA where the detection limit is 3.64 × 10−5 M. More importantly, the naked-eye detection limit of the paper-based sensor has been up to 11.45 ng/cm2. Also, theoretical calculation, and proposed mechanism, as well as the host-guest interactions have been carried out for the visual detection of PA. This study can provide a useful example of novel membrane sensors for explosives combining with the two weak luminescent systems based on the molecular design of covalent-nanocomposited emission enhancement (CNEE).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Picric acid
Fluorescent sensing
Nanofiber membrane
Electrospun

BK20150890 and BK20201389 ), Qing Lan Project of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (No. R2018Q03 ), and the Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of NUIST (No. 2014R002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption (a) and fluorescent emission (b) spectra of N-GQDs and Schiff-GQDs in ethanol solution; (c) The overlap between absorption spectra of PA and emission spectra of Schiff-GQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of dye 1 and Schiff-GQDs; (b) XPS full-spectra of N-GQDs and Schiff-GQDs; and C 1s (c), N 1s (d), O 1s (e), S 2p (f) core-level spectra of Schiff-GQDs.
(a) SEM image of electrospun Schiff-GQDs/PVA nanofiber films; (b–e) Mapping image of C, O, S and N elements, respectively; (f) Mapping image of N element after dripping PA.
AFM height image of Schiff-GQDs together with the diameter (a) and height (b).

PA titration experiments of N-GQDs (a, b, c) and Schiff-GQDs (d, e, f) in ethanol corresponding with UV–Vis absorption spectra (a, d), fluorescent titration spectra (b, e) and Stern-Volmer curve (c, f).

(a) Visual matrix image of paper-based Schiff-GQDs materials before and after addition of explosives; (b) Test strips for the visual detection of different concentrations of PA: original 0 M, a) 1.0 × 10−6 M, b) 5.0 × 10−6 M, c) 1.0 × 10−5 M, d) 1 × 10−4 M, e) 2 × 10−4 M, f) 4.4 × 10−4 M in the naked-eye and under UV illumination at 365 nm.

(a) Schematic diagram of electrospun; (b) Fluorescent spectra of electrospun Schiff-GQDs/PVA nanofiber films for PA titration; Inset: The change of fluorescent intensity at the maximum peak with different equivalents of PA; (c) Visual matrix image for the visual detection of different concentrations of PA vapors: original 0 M, a) 1.0 × 10−6 M, b) 5.0 × 10−6 M, c) 1.0 × 10−5 M, d) 1 × 10−4 M, e) 2 × 10−4 M, f) 4.4 × 10−4 M in the naked-eye and under UV illumination at 365 nm.

The visualized weak interaction for simplified models of PA@N-GQDs (a: top view, b: side view) and PA@Schiff-GQDs (c: top view, d: side view) by IGM analysis based on promolecular density (isovalue = 0.01). The color scale is given in a.u.
Schematic illustration for the sensitive detection of PA based on Schiff-GQDs.

head.ce:title: A Schiff base-functionalized graphene quantum dot nanocomposite for preferable picric acid sensing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science
Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology (NUIST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fast and effective picric acid (PA) sensing in water and the air is urgently demanded environmental safety. Herein, a new sensor was designed and synthesized mediated by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for explosive detection. The luminescent Schiff base-functionalized GQDs (Schiff-GQDs) was immobilized on nanocomposite through in-situ electrospun. The quantitative experiment shows that the fluorescent quantum yield of Schiff-GQDs is 11.63%, four times higher than that of N-GQDs (2.71%) and seven times higher than that of dye 1 (1.51%). Luminescent Schiff-GQDs have a bigger quenching percentage, better selective detection of PA where the detection limit is 3.64 × 10−5 M. More importantly, the naked-eye detection limit of the paper-based sensor has been up to 11.45 ng/cm2. Also, theoretical calculation, and proposed mechanism, as well as the host-guest interactions have been carried out for the visual detection of PA. This study can provide a useful example of novel membrane sensors for explosives combining with the two weak luminescent systems based on the molecular design of covalent-nanocomposited emission enhancement (CNEE).

ce:section.ce:para: Research on ultrasensitive detection and identification of explosives is of great significance for preventing and combating terrorist crimes, eliminating landmine threats, and protecting people's lives and environmental health [1–7]. Specifically, explosives' basic ingredients are 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, PA), sometimes with the addition of other nitroaromatic hydrocarbons [8]. Some high dynamite cannot exist without PA. According to the Occupational Safety and Health Administration, the order of explosive violence is PA > TNT >2,4-DNT [9]. On the one hand, PA is one of the most powerful and widely used explosives with low saturated vapor pressure and relatively high solubility in water [10–12]. On the other hand, PA becomes an important environmental pollutant due to its toxicity on the eyes, skin, liver, kidneys, and so on, where long-term intake can even bring permanent damage to human metabolism [13,14]. Therefore, from a safe and healthy perspective, it is of importance demand to developing sensitive, selective as well as fast-response to identify PA.

Many instrumental methods have been proposed for detecting nitroaromatic explosives, such as mass spectrometry [15], ion mobility spectroscopy [16–18], Raman spectroscopy [19–21], fluorescent spectroscopy [22–25], electrochemical analysis [8] and so on. One way to measure explosives is to measure the alternation of fluorescent properties between the host and guest molecules based on chemical sensors, which depends on their significant advantages, such as low price, high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, simple process, widely available compatibility and clear aggregation-induced emission (AIE) character [26]. However, how to design the fluorescent probes still is a challenging problem to realize rapid response and real-time monitoring not only in the laboratory but also outdoors [27]. More importantly, sensing materials for on-site detection need to meet the requirements of higher photostability and lower biological toxicity [28].

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted more interest in the field of advanced materials because of their fascinating properties, such as ultrahigh mechanical strength, high thermal stability, high conductivity, biocompatibility and so on, which makes them ideal chemical/biosensors and bioimaging materials [29,30]. However, GQDs show a smaller quantum yield than the traditional semiconducting quantum dots and fluorescent dyes, which is difficult to precisely control the emissive wavelength, especially the long-wavelength range. An obvious feature of GQDs is that the lateral size is greater than the thickness [31]. Different from all-inorganic quantum dots, GQDs not only fine control the periodic structures but also easily tune the preferable properties through introducing functional groups or reacting with surface groups [32–36]. For example, amino GQDs (N-GQDs) passivate the surface of GQDs, which often possess an aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) character. Functional groups, including amino, carboxyl and aldehyde, have produced the prospect of new functionalized GQDs for solid-state devices. Understanding the functional GQDs compatibility would be useful to realize the transition from ACQ to AIE.

Until now, there has been no report on the materials of aminated GQDs for the detection of nitroaromatics explosives. Herein, Schiff base-functionalized GQDs (Schiff-GQDs) were prepared from 5-(4-(diphenylamino)phenyl)thiophene-2-carbaldehyde (dye 1) and N-GQDs as raw materials using a facile solvothermal method. Then, the AIE-type quantum dots were uniformly immobilized on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) nanofibers through in-situ electrospun to fabricate the practical paper-based sensors (Scheme 1 ). Luminescent Schiff-GQDs have a high quenching percentage, high selective detection of PA where the detection limit is 3.64 × 10−5 M. Highly sensitive sensors of explosives in solution and solid media makes the functionalized GQDs have great potential for detecting PA in environmental monitoring.

Dye 1 [37] was from 5-bromothiophene-2-carbaldehyde and 4-triphenylamino boronic acid (Fig. S1). N-GQDs and explosives were purchased from commercial sources. Note: All reagents related to hazardous explosives can only be used in small amounts and be handled carefully. More details are to see supplementary data.

Dissolve N-GQDs (10 mg) and dye 1 (72.1 mg) in an appropriate amount of absolute ethanol (30 mL in total), and disperse them by ultrasound. Then, mixed the two compounds in a flask, add a small amount of glacial acetic acid as a catalyst, and the measured pH = 6. Add an appropriate amount of anhydrous sodium sulfate to remove water, heat to reflux (110 °C) under an argon atmosphere, and react for 16 h. Filter while hot to remove sodium sulfate, cool and stand overnight, precipitate in an ice bath, filter, and recrystallize with absolute ethanol. N-GQDs: FT–IR (KBr pellets, cm−1): 3435 (s), 2921 (s), 1645 (s), 1562 (s), 1448 (s), 1380 (s), and 1259 (s). Anal. calcd for CHN elements, Found: C, 46.96; H, 7.17; N, 18.86%. Schiff-GQDs: FT–IR (KBr pellets, cm−1): 3384 (s), 2921 (s), 1640 (s), 1562 (s), 1448 (s), 1380 (s), 1259 (s), 1121 (vs), and 616 (s). Anal. calcd for CHN elements, Found: C, 65.03; H, 5.63; N, 10.80%.

Schiff-GQDs and 10% PVA aqueous solution were stirred at 80 °C for 12 h to prepare an electrospun solution with 0.05 wt% Schiff-GQDs. Put this solution in a syringe, discharge the bubbles, connect the needle to the positive high voltage line and stick the aluminum foil to the receiving roller. Optimized conditions: the electrospun voltage is 13 kV; the receiving distance is 10 cm; the speed of the sliding table is 15.0 mm/s; the liquid supply rate is 0.01 mL/h; the temperature is 34 °C; and the receiving time is 8–12 h to prepare the electrospun film. The prepared electrospun film is placed in a vacuum drying oven and dried at 60 °C to remove residual solvent.

As shown in Scheme S1, Schiff-GQDs were prepared from dye 1 and N-GQDs. The facile synthesis and potential photophysical properties of dye 1 are the reason for choosing this precursor. Compound 1 shows an ACQ character in Fig. S2. Two negatives make a positive. The emission will be released if the chromophore is monodispersed after entering a microenvironment. Therefore, the materials marrying dye 1 and N-GQDs stand at a local unit to achieve the transformation from ACQ to AIE.

UV–Vis and fluorescent spectra of N-GQDs and Schiff-GQDs have been present in Fig. 1 for comparison. At the same time, N-GQDs show excitation-independent photoluminescent (PL) behavior with an emission wavelength at 470 nm in Fig. S3. Specifically, N-GQDs exhibit an absorption peak at 350 nm, while Schiff-GQDs have a weak absorption partly owing to the low solubility in ethanol. However, a clear PL enhancement is observed from N-GQDs to Schiff-GQDs in Fig. 1b and Fig. S4. The quantitative experiment (see supplementary data) shows that the fluorescent quantum yield of Schiff-GQDs is 11.63%, four times higher than that of N-GQDs (2.71%) and seven times higher than that of dye 1 (1.51%). Besides, the large cross-sectional area also is measured between UV–Vis spectra of PA and PL spectra of Schiff-GQDs, suggesting that the Schiff-GQDs with versatile N- and O-element surface states have great potentials in detecting PA by host-guest interactions [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT−IR and XPS analysis of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further analyze the chemical composition of N-GQDs and Schiff-GQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Infrared (FT−IR) have been carried out. Fig. 2 a shows the FT−IR spectra of dye 1 and Schiff-GQDs for comparison. The absorption peak at 1661 cm−1 is ascribed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the carbonyl CO bonds for dye 1, which completely disappeared for Schiff-GQDs. Simultaneously, the profile of Schiff-GQDs exhibits a characteristic absorption of imine stretching vibration at 1640 cm− 1, indicating the formation of CN bonds for the Schiff base.

From XPS full-spectra in Fig. 2b, firstly, there is an obvious S 2p peak on Schiff-GQDs, while no S 2p peak on N-GQDs, which indicates that the source of S 2p is the thiophene unit on dye 1. Secondly, the intensity of C 1s peak for Schiff-GQDs is higher than that for N-GQDs, while the relative intensities of N 1s and O 1s remain unchanged. Also, the 1s core-level spectra of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements, as well as S 2p spectra have been shown in Fig. 2c–f for further analysis between N-GQDs and Schiff-GQDs samples.

Different from the XPS data of N-GQDs in Fig. S5 [39], the presence of the characteristic CN bonds in Schiff-GQDs has been confirmed again. The C 1s core-level spectra (Fig. 2c) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.8, 285.7, 286.8 and 288.2 eV, respectively, which are attributed to the carbon atoms from C–C/CC bonds, C–N bonds, C–O/C–S bonds and CO/CN bonds, respectively. More importantly, The N 1s spectra reveal the existence of N–C bonds (399.5 eV) and NC bonds (398.8 eV, Fig. 2d). The O 1s spectra exhibit three peaks at 531.3, 532.6 and 533.5 eV, respectively, from OC bonds, O–C bonds, and OC–O bonds (Fig. 2e). Interestingly, the relative peak area at 531.3 eV is from 53.8% of N-GQDs to 43.5% of Schiff-GQDs, showing a decreasing percentage. As illustrated in the S 2p (Fig. 2f), the spectra reveal the double peaks at 164.5 and 163.3 eV for Schiff-GQDs, corresponding to the S species with the normal spin−orbit coupling effects (J = 1.2 eV) in a peak area ratio of approximately 1:2, indicative of the existence of C–S–C [40,41]. Finally, XPS data further proves that dye 1 has successfully been cross-linked with N-GQDs to form Schiff-GQDs with fluorescent functional groups.

Generally speaking, there is a different performance for detection of explosives between in the solutions and in the solid states. In practice, intrinsic materials were fabricated by the electrospun method [42–47] to form the nanocomposites for paper-based sensors and for nanofiber membranes. As shown in Fig. 3 a, SEM images of electrospun Schiff-GQDs/PVA nanofiber films show a clear network structure with a diameter of approximately 50 nm. PVA contains C, H and O elements, and N-GQDs contains C, H, N and O elements, as well as Schiff-GQDs contains C, H, N, O and S elements. Remarkably, mapping images of C, O, N and S elements exhibit a uniform distribution in Fig. 3b–e. Therefore, the sulfur element is only from the thiophene rings in Schiff-GQDs, which suggests dye 1 has successfully been linked with N-GQDs employing covalent and/or non-covalent ways. Interestingly, the highlights of the N mapping image have an increasing phenomenon after dripping PA because of the nitro groups.

As shown in Fig. 4 , atomic force microscopy (AFM) was employed to further investigate the morphology of Schiff-GQDs. The films keep the structure and characteristics of normal GQDs. However, two domain kinds of GQDs have been observed based on tapping mode, showing two peaks with the height of approximately 10 nm and 18 nm, partly since GQDs possess a different number of graphene layers. After analyzing the data of relative height Δh, the Δh is a constant value of 1.5 nm. Amazingly, the length of dye 1 approximately is 1.5 nm. Then, we have tried to attribute the relative height to dye 1. In addition, SEM images (Fig. S6 and S7, see supplementary data) also are a shred of important evidence. All have been shown that Schiff-GQDs, a covalent nanocomposite from dye 1 and GQDs, have buried themselves deep in the PVA membrane.

The specific luminescent mechanism of GQDs has always been controversial. However, the fascinating properties of GQDs continue to inspire researchers to develop new applications [48]. Herein, the blue emissive Schiff-GQDs are used for selective detection of PA not only in solution but also in paper-based sensors, as well as in nanofiber membranes.

N-GQDs and Schiff-GQDs have been used for the detection of PA in Fig. 5 . PA is continuously dripped into ethanol solution with a probe molecule concentration of 0.025 mg/mL, and the fluorescent emission gradually weakened when the concentration of PA is 2.5 × 10−4 mol/L. According to the Stern-Volmer equation, the quenching constants K sv of N-GQDs and Schiff-GQDs are 2.14 × 104 M−1 and 1.73 × 104 M−1, respectively. Simultaneously, the detection limit of N-GQDs is estimated to be 8.13 × 10−5 M, while that of Schiff-GQDs is 3.64 × 10−5 M. It can also be observed that the sensitivity of Schiff-GQDs fluorescent probe to PA is slightly better than that of N-GQDs in solution.

Fig. 5e shows that the fluorescent emission peak is red-shifted from 440 to 465 nm with the increase of PA concentration, since PA interacts with the electron-donating group in Schiff-GQDs, resulting in enhanced charge transfer in the entire molecule. The results of the spectral analysis also prove this view. The absorption of PA and the emission of Schiff-GQDs overlap significantly at 370–500 nm in Fig. 1c. After the PA is in contact with the fluorescent probe Schiff-GQDs, an efficient energy transfer phenomenon occurs, which causes the fluorescent quenching of Schiff-GQDs. The strength is much lower than when it exists alone. According to the above discussion, the highly efficient fluorescent quenching of PA on Schiff-GQDs comes from the dual fluorescent sensing mechanism by energy transfer and charge transfer.

More importantly, the paper-based sensor Schiff-GQDs materials have been fabricated using the self-assembly method for the visual detection of PA by immobilization on filter paper (Fig. 6 ). Cut the cheap and portable test paper into several pieces of 1.0 × 1.0 cm2 size, fully immerse them in the Schiff-GQDs solution with 1.0 mg/mL dose, take out and dry them to make a fluorescent test paper sensor for ultrafast detection. The original fiber test paper is light yellow under visible light conditions, while it exhibits a certain fluorescent emission under UV light. After contacting different explosive molecules, the test paper shows different color changes. In particular, the fluorescent and color changes of PA are the most obvious. Fig. 6b shows the test paper detecting PA explosives of different concentrations (4.4 × 10−4–1.0 × 10−6 M). As the PA concentration gradually increases, the concentration of the fluorescent test paper gradually declines. The color changes significantly under UV light irradiation, and the detection limit visible to the naked-eye reaches 11.45 ng/cm2. These experimental results show that Schiff-GQDs have great application potential in the field of rapid detection of trace PA explosives.

The Schiff-GQDs/PVA nanofiber membrane is prepared by electrospun in Fig. 7 a. As previously mentioned, the stable nanofiber membrane has a larger porosity and specific surface area, which provides a microenvironment and diffusion path for explosives [49]. Fluorescent titration shows that when the concentration of PA drops is 5.0 × 10−5 M, the fluorescent quenching efficiency of the fiber membrane fluorescent sensor reaches 46.4%. When the concentration of PA is 2.5 × 10−4 M, the fluorescent quenching efficiency reaches 82.5% (Fig. 7b). As the concentration of PA gradually increases, the concentration of the fluorescent nanofiber membrane gradually declines in Fig. S8. More importantly, the test of PA vapors has also been conducted at the mouth of a bottle with various concentrations of PA. As illustrated in Fig. 7c, the original nanofiber remains unchanged under visible light conditions. However, the film exhibits a certain fluorescent quenching from light blue to dark blue under UV light. From a quantitative point of view, the detection limit of its naked-eye recognition is 1.0 × 10−6 M, superior to the result of PA drops. The film is white in the naked-eye that would be interpreted as a stable and non-reactive material. Therefore, the emissive alternation is largely due to the supramolecular interactions.

The geometries of the simplified model for PA@N-GQDs and PA@Schiff-GQDs were fully calculated at the B3LYP-D3(BJ)/6-311+G(d,p) level [50–54]. The optimized geometries (see Table S1) show that the PA is parallel to the plane of the graphene fragment in two complexes in the form of a carbon atom in the graphene almost faces to the center of the benzene unit in PA, and the vertical distances between the molecular units are 3.52 and 3.33 Å in PA@N-GQDs and PA@Schiff-GQDs, respectively. The complexation energies of PA@N-GQDs and PA@Schiff-GQDs are calculated to be −21.72 and −23.61 kcal/mol, respectively, which shows a large enough discrimination.

The independent gradient model (IGM) analysis can visually provide valuable information about the weak interaction between molecular units. Fig. 8 shows the visualized weak interaction between PA and two GQDs by IGM analysis based on promolecular density. There is a large area of flat green isosurface between the molecular units for two complexes, revealing the low electron density in these regions and weak interaction between the structural units of the complexes, because of the π−π van der Waals (vdW) attraction between PA and GQDs. This can also be confirmed by the distance between segments (3.52 and 3.33 Å, respectively) discussed above which can be regarded as π−π stacking distance. No strong attraction (blue isosurface) or strong repulsion (red isosurface) was observed in the two complexes. More importantly, the IGM isosurface region of complex PA@Schiff-GQDs is wider than that of PA@N-GQDs, which explains why the vdW interaction of the former is stronger than that of the latter. These results are consistent with fluorescent titration experiments.

In summary, two fluorescent probes, N-GQDs and Schiff-GQDs, have been successfully prepared for the effective detection of explosives. Interestingly, the fluorescent quantum yield of Schiff-GQDs is 11.63%, four times higher than that of N-GQDs (2.71%) and seven times higher than that of dye 1 (1.51%). We think this is a phenomenon of Covalent-Nanocomposited Emission Enhancement (CNEE). Two negatives, dye 1 with ACQ and N-GQDs with weak fluorescence, make a positive, largely since the emission would be released from ACQ to AIE if the chromophore is monodispersed after entering the platform of Schiff-GQDs based on quantum confinement effect.

Based on molecular design, the blue emissive Schiff-GQDs are used for the detection of PA not only in solution but also in paper-based sensors, as well as in nanofiber membranes. First of all, luminescent Schiff-GQDs have a high quenching percentage, high selective detection of PA where the detection limit is 3.64 × 10−5 M. Secondly, the naked-eye detection limit of the paper-based sensor has been up to 11.45 ng/cm2. Thirdly, from a quantitative point of view, with the gradual increase of the PA concentration, the fluorescent of the recognition nanofiber film is gradually quenched, and the color gradually changes to navy blue. The detection limit of its naked-eye recognition is 1.0 × 10−6 M. Therefore, it has great potential as a chemical sensor for explosive vapors. This study can provide a useful method on fluorescent membrane sensors not only for achieving the transformation from ACQ to AIE but also for developing the future CNEE materials in the solid device.

Yuting Fan: Wrote original draft and experimented. Tao Tao: Contributed to the conceptualization of the study and wrote the manuscript. Huijun Wang: Contributed to the data analysis and figure design. Zeyu Liu: Contributed to the theoretical calculation and manuscript preparation. Wei Huang: Contributed to the experimental instruction. Hui Cao: Contributed to the conception of the study and manuscript preparation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21501097), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Nos. BK20150890 and BK20201389), Qing Lan Project of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (No. R2018Q03), and the Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of NUIST (No. 2014R002).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109355.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) from explosive wastewater using nanoscale zero-valent iron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang X, Lin YM, Shan XQ, Chen ZL. Degradation of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) from explosive wastewater using nanoscale zero-valent iron. Chem Eng J 2010;158(3):566-570.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient solid-state emission of diphenylfumaronitriles with full-color AIE, and application in explosive sensing, data storage and WLEDs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu DB, Gong WJ, Yao HM, Huang LM, Lin ZH, Ling QD. Highly efficient solid-state emission of diphenylfumaronitriles with full-color AIE, and application in explosive sensing, data storage and WLEDs. Dyes Pigments 2020;172:107829.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitroaromatic detection and infrared communication from wildtype plants using plant nanobionics

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective sensing of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) in aqueous media with "aggregation-induced emission enhancement'' (AIEE)-active iridium(III) complexes
